data_licences: Open Government Licence – Canada (Environment and Climate Change Canada)

source.primary: Lake Ontario (sole source) — intake depth 6–9m, 1–3km offshore
source.wfi_note: Great Lakes source — no wildfire interface risk
source.lake_ontario_note: Lake Ontario levels managed by IJC Plan 2014. Current level: 75.37m IGLD 1985.

lake_ontario.name: Lake Ontario (de facto reservoir)
lake_ontario.data_note: Lake Ontario has ~1,639 km³ of water — effectively unlimited storage.

storage.data_note: Lake Ontario provides essentially unlimited supply. Toronto Water's constraint is treatment capacity and distribution infrastructure, not source volume.

plants.name: R.C. Harris WTP
plants.process: conventional coagulation/sedimentation/filtration, UV, chlorination, fluoride. Built 1941.

plants.name: Island WTP
plants.process: conventional + UV + chlorination + fluoride. Serves Toronto Island.

treatment.data_note: Total treatment capacity ~2,600 ML/day for a city using ~700 ML/day — significant redundancy.

restriction.hypothesized_triggers: Toronto has a seasonal outdoor watering bylaw (odd/even by address). Full restrictions rare given Great Lakes source.

snowpack.note: Toronto's supply is from Lake Ontario, not snowmelt-dependent watersheds.

risk.single_point_of_failure: None at source level — Lake Ontario is effectively unlimited. Distribution network age (many pipes pre-1950) is the primary infrastructure risk.
risk.climate_note: Lake Ontario levels are above long-term average (75.37m vs historical mean). Climate risk is increasing algae and turbidity events near intakes.

use.data_note: Toronto has one of the lowest per-capita uses of any major Canadian city.

egress.cso_note: Combined sewers in older (pre-1950) neighbourhoods. Wet weather flow management program ongoing.
egress.data_note: Toronto is one of few Canadian cities with tertiary wastewater treatment — phosphorus removal before Lake Ontario discharge.
